news_summary: Efforts to improve living conditions for slum dwellers and empower disabled Palestinian athletes demonstrate progress in dignity and inclusion.
news_summary_fr: Les efforts visant à améliorer les conditions de vie des habitants des bidonvilles et à autonomiser les athlètes palestiniens en situation de handicap témoignent des progrès accomplis en matière de dignité et d'inclusion.
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas para mejorar las condiciones de vida de los habitantes de los barrios marginales y para empoderar a los deportistas palestinos con discapacidad ponen de manifiesto avances en materia de dignidad e inclusión.

articles.title: 1.1 billion people live in slums. Can they be housed in dignity?
articles.summary: Ministers, mayors, international organisations, urban planners and experts are in Baku, Azerbaijan, to tackle one of the world’s fastest-growing challenges: the global housing crisis, which, according to the United Nations, affects nearly 2.8 billion people worldwide.
articles.content:
How can cities be made safer and more resilient, and how can housing be made more affordable? These are the questions under discussion at the thirteenth session of the World Urban Forum (WUF13), which opened on Sunday.

Over the past decade, many countries have made visible progress: around 160 countries have already adopted or are developing national urban policies, while more than two-thirds of countries have introduced housing affordability programmes.

However, according to the UN Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat), these efforts remain insufficient. More than 1.1 billion people live in slums or informal settlements worldwide, and more than 120 million people over the last decade were either born into or moved into slums and informal settlements.

Recovery from war and destruction

Solutions such as expanded social housing programmes, improvements to informal settlements and protection for vulnerable populations were discussed during the opening sessions of the week-long event.

Special attention was given to countries recovering from war and destruction. Bashar Al Sebaai, mayor of Homs, Syria, which suffered extensive damage during years of conflict, says that the city urgently needs not only ideas and expertise, but also financing to restore basic services and infrastructure.

“400,000 returned to the city after the war,” he told UN News. “They came back to severely damaged neighbourhoods. Solid waste, infrastructure, electricity…it’s very hard to find solution to all these problems.”

Climate Change and Cities

The millions living in unsafe housing are among the first to suffer from floods, heatwaves and other extreme events that are being made worse as a result of the man-made climate crisis.

UN News/ Nargiz Shekinskaya

The construction sector remains one of the world’s largest sources of greenhouse gas emissions, and low-carbon construction, resilient urban planning and climate-sensitive upgrading of informal settlements are on the agenda.

The first day of the forum itself was marked by prolonged heavy rain in Baku, forcing city authorities to take urgent measures to drain flooded roads. Local residents noted that such weather events were rare in Azerbaijan just a few years ago, especially at this time of year.

Homelessness: A crisis for rich and poor nations

“When we have a homeless population of hundreds of thousands of people in the streets in the United States, and we’re considered to be wealthy…In New York, it looks like a crisis right now,” Lance Jay Brown, the founder of the Consortium for Sustainable Urbanization, told UN News.

Mr. Brown noted that, during his lifetime, the world’s population has nearly quadrupled, while affordable housing for low-income communities has become increasingly difficult to secure.

The US-based architect expressed hope that the discussions and decisions emerging from Baku would help generate practical solutions to the global housing crisis.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant global challenge—housing for over 1.1 billion people living in slums—and reports on a major international forum focused on finding practical, large-scale solutions to improve housing dignity, affordability, and urban resilience. It addresses broad societal impact, includes detailed context on policy progress, challenges, and climate considerations, and emphasizes efforts to support vulnerable populations worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Global leaders meet in Baku to tackle housing for 1.1 billion slum dwellers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Nearly 2.8 billion people worldwide face housing challenges, with over 1.1 billion living in slums or informal settlements. This growing problem affects cities everywhere, making safe and affordable housing a top priority.

**What happened** – From June 25, 2024, ministers, mayors, experts, and international organizations gathered in Baku, Azerbaijan, for the thirteenth World Urban Forum (WUF13). They discussed how to improve housing affordability, make cities safer, and support people living in damaged or informal homes, especially in places recovering from war like Homs, Syria.

**Impact** – The forum highlights progress made by about 160 countries adopting urban policies and many creating housing programs. Still, challenges remain as millions live in unsafe conditions, exposed to climate risks like floods and heatwaves. The event also focused on reducing construction pollution and upgrading homes to be stronger against climate change.

**What's next step** – The discussions aim to create practical plans and gather funding to rebuild cities, expand social housing, and protect vulnerable groups worldwide. The forum encourages global cooperation to turn ideas into action and help millions improve their living conditions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Leaders and experts are working together in Baku to find real solutions for the housing crisis affecting over a billion people living in slums around the world.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des dirigeants mondiaux réunis à Bakou pour loger dignement 1,1 milliard de personnes vivant dans des bidonvilles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Près de 2,8 milliards de personnes dans le monde rencontrent des difficultés de logement, dont plus de 1,1 milliard vivent dans des bidonvilles ou des habitats informels. Ce problème croissant touche de nombreuses villes, rendant le logement sûr et abordable une priorité.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – À partir du 25 juin 2024, ministres, maires, experts et organisations internationales se sont réunis à Bakou, en Azerbaïdjan, pour le treizième Forum mondial sur l’urbanisme (WUF13). Ils ont discuté des moyens d’améliorer l’accès au logement abordable, de rendre les villes plus sûres et de soutenir les populations vivant dans des logements endommagés ou informels, notamment dans des zones en reconstruction comme Homs, en Syrie.

**Impact** – Le forum met en lumière les progrès réalisés par environ 160 pays qui ont adopté des politiques urbaines et créé des programmes de logement. Pourtant, des défis subsistent, car des millions de personnes vivent dans des conditions dangereuses, exposées aux risques climatiques tels que les inondations et les vagues de chaleur. L’événement a aussi abordé la réduction de la pollution liée à la construction et l’amélioration des logements pour les rendre plus résistants au changement climatique.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les discussions visent à élaborer des plans concrets et à mobiliser des financements pour reconstruire les villes, développer le logement social et protéger les populations vulnérables dans le monde. Le forum encourage la coopération internationale pour transformer les idées en actions et améliorer les conditions de vie de millions de personnes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des dirigeants et experts travaillent ensemble à Bakou pour trouver des solutions concrètes à la crise du logement qui touche plus d’un milliard de personnes vivant dans des bidonvilles à travers le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Líderes mundiales se reúnen en Bakú para mejorar la vivienda de 1.100 millones en barrios pobres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Casi 2.800 millones de personas en el mundo enfrentan problemas de vivienda, y más de 1.100 millones viven en barrios pobres o asentamientos informales. Este problema creciente afecta a muchas ciudades, haciendo que la vivienda segura y asequible sea una prioridad.

**Qué pasó** – Desde el 25 de junio de 2024, ministros, alcaldes, expertos y organizaciones internacionales se reunieron en Bakú, Azerbaiyán, para el decimotercer Foro Mundial Urbano (WUF13). Discutieron cómo mejorar la vivienda asequible, hacer las ciudades más seguras y apoyar a las personas que viven en hogares dañados o informales, especialmente en lugares que se recuperan de la guerra como Homs, Siria.

**Impacto** – El foro destaca los avances de unos 160 países que han adoptado políticas urbanas y creado programas de vivienda. Sin embargo, aún quedan desafíos porque millones viven en condiciones inseguras, expuestos a riesgos climáticos como inundaciones y olas de calor. El evento también se centró en reducir la contaminación de la construcción y mejorar las viviendas para que sean más resistentes al cambio climático.

**Próximos pasos** – Las discusiones buscan crear planes prácticos y reunir fondos para reconstruir ciudades, ampliar la vivienda social y proteger a los grupos vulnerables en todo el mundo. El foro fomenta la cooperación global para convertir ideas en acciones y ayudar a millones a mejorar sus condiciones de vida.

**Resumen en una frase** – Líderes y expertos trabajan juntos en Bakú para encontrar soluciones reales a la crisis de vivienda que afecta a más de mil millones de personas que viven en barrios pobres en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sprawling cityscape blending fragile, colorful slum-like homes with sturdy, rising buildings under construction, connected by glowing pathways symbolizing hope and resilience, with gentle rain nourishing the scene and soft light breaking through clouds to represent recovery, community support, and sustainable urban growth.

articles.title: Cannes: Palestinian paracycling crew formed by amputee athletes from Gaza arrive
articles.summary: Members of "Gaza Sunbirds", a Palestinian paracycling crew formed by amputee athletes from Gaza, made a symbolic arrival by sea at the Cannes Film Festival for the screening of a documentary about them.
articles.content:
One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site.

Try again
articles.source: News and stories on CULTURE, movies, music, literature - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a Palestinian paracycling crew of amputee athletes from Gaza participating symbolically at an international event, promoting visibility and empowerment for disabled individuals in a conflict-affected region. This represents a meaningful social and human rights achievement with broad inspirational impact.
articles.personality_title: Palestinian paracycling team from Gaza arrives at Cannes to share their story
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gaza is a region where many people face difficult living conditions, including those who have lost limbs due to conflict. Sports for people with disabilities are rare there but can offer hope and strength.

**What happened** – A group of amputee athletes from Gaza, called the "Gaza Sunbirds," formed a paracycling team. They arrived by sea at the Cannes Film Festival to attend the showing of a documentary about their lives and sports journey.

**Impact** – This event gave the Gaza Sunbirds a chance to be seen and heard on an international stage. It highlights the abilities and courage of disabled people living in tough situations, showing that they can achieve remarkable things despite challenges.

**What's next step** – The documentary and the team's presence at Cannes may encourage more support for disabled sports in Gaza and inspire others in similar situations to pursue their goals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A paracycling team of amputee athletes from Gaza gained international attention at Cannes, sharing their powerful story through a documentary.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une équipe palestinienne de paracyclisme de Gaza arrive à Cannes pour raconter leur histoire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Gaza est une région où beaucoup de personnes vivent dans des conditions difficiles, notamment celles ayant perdu des membres à cause du conflit. Le sport pour les personnes handicapées y est rare, mais il peut apporter espoir et force.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un groupe d’athlètes amputés de Gaza, appelé « Gaza Sunbirds », a formé une équipe de paracyclisme. Ils sont arrivés par la mer au Festival de Cannes pour assister à la projection d’un documentaire sur leur vie et leur parcours sportif.

**Impact** – Cet événement a permis aux Gaza Sunbirds d’être vus et entendus sur une scène internationale. Il met en lumière les capacités et le courage des personnes handicapées vivant dans des situations difficiles, montrant qu’elles peuvent accomplir de grandes choses malgré les obstacles.

**Prochaine étape** – Le documentaire et la présence de l’équipe à Cannes pourraient encourager plus de soutien au sport pour personnes handicapées à Gaza et inspirer d’autres personnes dans des situations similaires à poursuivre leurs objectifs.

**Une phrase clé** – Une équipe de paracyclisme d’athlètes amputés de Gaza a attiré l’attention internationale à Cannes en partageant leur histoire forte à travers un documentaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Equipo palestino de paraciclismo de Gaza llega a Cannes para contar su historia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Gaza es una región donde muchas personas viven en condiciones difíciles, incluyendo quienes han perdido extremidades debido al conflicto. El deporte para personas con discapacidad es poco común allí, pero puede brindar esperanza y fuerza.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de atletas amputados de Gaza, llamado "Gaza Sunbirds", formó un equipo de paraciclismo. Llegaron por mar al Festival de Cine de Cannes para asistir a la proyección de un documental sobre sus vidas y su recorrido deportivo.

**Impacto** – Este evento dio a los Gaza Sunbirds la oportunidad de ser vistos y escuchados en un escenario internacional. Resalta las habilidades y el valor de las personas con discapacidad que viven en situaciones difíciles, mostrando que pueden lograr cosas notables pese a los desafíos.

**Próximo paso** – El documental y la presencia del equipo en Cannes podrían fomentar más apoyo al deporte para discapacitados en Gaza e inspirar a otros en situaciones similares a seguir sus metas.

**Una frase clave** – Un equipo de paraciclismo de atletas amputados de Gaza ganó atención internacional en Cannes al compartir su poderosa historia a través de un documental.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of three stylized, abstract bicycles with one wheel shaped like a crescent and another wheel formed by a hand in a gesture of strength, set against a softly textured backdrop inspired by the Gaza coastline, using natural earthy tones and gentle light to evoke resilience and unity without showing any human figures.
